output: Mr. President, first of all, I would like to congratulate Mr. Siebeck on the work he has carried out, as his report reflects many of the concerns expressed here...
Focus on these critical issues facing the entire European Union. They are water scarcity and drought, matters that have already ceased to be solely problems of southern European countries.

I am pleased that this project incorporates some of the reflections contained in the opinion report I presented before the Committee on Agriculture, advocating for agricultural activity and, for example, highlighting the role played by farmers in the sustainable management of available resources.

It also includes a reference to drought and water scarcity as factors exacerbating raw material prices—an aspect, in today’s context, that I also consider very important to highlight, so that we keep in mind not only the environmental dimension of this issue, but also some of its most significant economic consequences.

The Environment Committee, furthermore, included the proposal to establish a European Drought Observatory, an initiative mentioned in the Commission's Agriculture report, which I hope will one day be reflected in concrete actions.

The text scheduled for voting tomorrow, however, does not include a proposal made by the Committee on Agriculture calling for an examination of establishing an economic adaptation fund to combat drought, a fund that would benefit all economic sectors, including agriculture.

On my part, I would like to make it clear that I will continue to defend the establishment of this fund, an idea that I will once again propose to Parliament when, in the coming months, the Commission publishes its communication on adapting to climate change.

It seems to me that, after the establishment of the Solidarity Fund—which was originally created to alleviate losses caused by climatic disasters—it is now time to consider an instrument that acts in advance and is specifically dedicated to financing preventive measures aimed at reducing the environmental and economic costs of these climatic catastrophes.

Mr. President, a large majority of farmers and livestock producers are awaiting decisions and prior agreements that the Council of Ministers for Agriculture of the Union will adopt between today and tomorrow regarding this review of the Union.

the CAP, because this is not merely a routine review but would bring profound changes, such as the disappearance of numerous farms, if the European Commission's proposed cut to direct payments is approved.

Modulation is the central issue of this parliamentary report and could be one of the few elements of the Parliament's opinion with any real impact on the negotiations being conducted by the Union's Ministers of Agriculture.
We have been opposing for years any increase in what we euphemistically call modulation, because if the rural development policy does not have sufficient funds, it is not...
not due to a lack of resources within the budget ceiling of the CAP, but due to an absolute absence of political will to provide the community budget with that necessary pillar.
Do you want to liberalize the markets?
Of course, do so, but with identical requirements and conditions for all producers, both within and outside the Union.

At this moment, the European Union is determining the future of numerous farmers and livestock producers, and I would particularly like to refer to vulnerable sectors such as sheep, cattle, and tobacco, whose survival in many producing regions will depend on this review.

Some producers, such as those in sheep farming, no longer seek assistance but simply wish to be allowed to exit the market with dignity, as they have done the calculations and recognize that the only way to survive is to reduce production volumes.

Abandoning production, therefore, is the only option we leave to many producers, whose market space will undoubtedly be filled by imports from third countries, as consumers will not cease consuming and the world will continue to increase the number of consumers at an ever-accelerating pace.
The Parliament should issue a prudent opinion, and the Council should listen to it.

I would like to express my gratitude here to the President of the Committee on Agriculture for the efforts made by Mr. Paris to ensure that the Parliament is heard and not so clearly and blatantly disregarded by the Council and the Commission as has been the case until now.

If we do it well, we will be laying the foundations for a common agricultural policy beyond 2013. If not, we will be bringing about the demise of many European farmers.

I disagree with allowing parents to participate in co-financing, because the same old problems will occur. Parents who can contribute money will ensure their children in schools consume fruits and vegetables, while those who cannot will be left out of the program.
I believe it must be a community-funded plan in order for its distribution to be uniform.

It's not merely about a student eating an apple, but about knowing what kind of apple they are eating, understanding its nutritional properties, and comprehending the beneficial effects that this fruit has on their health and development.
Of course, they must be products of guaranteed quality, giving priority, if possible, to seasonal fruits, and whenever feasible, I advocate for the Community origin of our fruits and vegetables.

I believe we will immediately notice the beneficial effects of this plan, which I commend and to which I wish to extend my congratulations to the Commissioner, for the present and future health of our children and their education.

Mr. President, on behalf of the Socialist Group, I wish to express the strongest condemnation by my Group of the killings being carried out in this region of Somalia in the name of God.
Few atrocities have been committed by someone who did not invoke the name of God or of a religion.

Specifically, the manner in which this atrocious murder was carried out—we already know—was a 13-year-old girl who was raped and then charged with adultery, while five men, if they can even be called men, rushed to stone her to death, even preventing anyone from attempting to save her in a stadium filled with a thousand people.
This, combined with the acts of piracy this year off the coast of Somalia, which have reached nearly one hundred ships seized, constitutes a humanitarian situation that is utterly unacceptable.
The international community cannot remain indifferent to this.
It cannot remain indifferent to cowardice, to the invocation of religion to commit barbarities.
Therefore, we must restore order by supporting the legitimate Government of Somalia in regaining control over the entire country and establishing a state of law that respects human rights.
I believe that rarely have we been faced with a situation so clear-cut and one that imposes such obligations.
I do not believe we can remain indifferent here in this community of 500 million people and 27 countries, the most important in the world, without intervening in this situation.
I don't know how it can be done, but certainly I believe it must be done and that action must be taken quickly.

Of course, we must ensure that the opposition can carry out its role; we must oppose fraud, but we must also defend the actions of NGOs.

Pest and disease control affecting the cultivation of a wide variety of food crops, specifically all Mediterranean products, as well as the cultivation of ornamental plants and cut flowers.

Mr. President, Mr. Capato, your report is full of goodwill. The most evident proof of this is that it received no amendments during its consideration by the Committee on Liberties.
It was you yourself who introduced eighteen amendments to your own report.
And those amendments are what have imparted to the report a greater degree of radicalism, so to speak, in general terms, which raises very serious doubts in my mind.
I would categorize the amendments you have proposed—amendments you have proposed to yourself, I insist—in four distinct sections, in a report that initially received support from all groups.
Some are impossible. You are asking for some things that are impossible.

For example, Mr. Capato, you are requesting measures for which there is still no legal basis. For example, as long as the Treaty of Lisbon has not entered into force, it will be impossible to harmonize the regulations of the various commissions.
something similar to what Commissioner Wallström has just stated. For example, you request that we declare 2009 the year of transparency. We are already late; the deadline has passed.
Therefore, you are asking for things that are impossible.
Moreover, it requests some others that are inadvisable.
For example, you request transparency in the meetings of political groups.
No, less.
that privacy is needed. Privacy is a political good.
If I wish to disagree with my party, to maintain opposition, I want to do so in private, and that is not a lack of transparency.
Transparency must be demanded in public institutions, but not within political parties, where debates must be guaranteed within privacy.
Therefore, I cannot follow you on that.
There are some other assessments you make, Mr. Capato, that are uncertain.
For example, you state that ninety percent of legislative activity is related to comitology.
That is a reckless exaggeration, Mr. Capato.
Moreover, criminology inherently entails standards of transparency in itself.
If now what we do is create more mechanisms, more bureaucracy, more controls, we will be moving against what we aim to achieve, which is transparency—something that closely resembles simplicity.
Finally, before Mr. President calls me to attention for taking up too much of my time, there are a few things that are unnecessary.
For example, I understand that it is very wet and very appealing to public opinion to demand transparency in the activities of deputies, such as knowing whether they dance during plenary sessions.
As is already known, Mr. Capato.
or that goes to the committees, it is already known that if there is a record of all of them, these acts are accessible to all citizens.
or the diets they collect. As everyone knows, Mr. Capato.
That is, it is entirely possible for a citizen to gain access to that information.

Therefore, I understand that it may appear very weak in the eyes of public opinion and that it greatly enhances the image to defend transparency. But I want you to know that transparency means making things appear as they truly are. And when one contributes to obscuring how things actually are, Mr. Capato, then we are not contributing to transparency.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, as we see, in recent times the textile sector has been undergoing a significant crisis, leading to numerous business closures, relocations, and unemployment—events that are precisely concentrated in regions specialized in this activity.

Given this current economic crisis context, the European Commission should act swiftly alongside the Member States to alleviate the socio-economic impacts of this restructuring, particularly devastating for the affected territories and families.

I believe it is necessary to provide support to workers in the textile and clothing sector through social measures, as well as through plans for companies undergoing restructuring and currently facing serious difficulties.
It would be desirable to allocate a substantial portion of the Globalization Adjustment Fund to the restructuring and reskilling of the textile sector.
particularly small and medium-sized enterprises, which make up the majority of the sector in the European Union and are most severely affected by market liberalization.
On the other hand, it is necessary to reinstate the system for controlling imports coming from abroad, and especially those originating from China due to their volume.
It is in no way intended to promote trade barriers.

It aims to offset the negative effects of such a significant transformation. Indeed, we must also remember that the European Union is the world's second-largest exporter of textile products and garments.

That is, ensuring the best possible access conditions to third-country markets becomes necessary, which are essential for the future of the textile and clothing industry based in the European Union, and particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises.
All of this, of course, always guaranteeing fair competition based on the promotion of environmental and social standards in these countries.

In this regard, the publication of accurate information to consumers, for example through the regulation on the "Made in" labeling, which as far as we know has not yet been implemented, would be highly useful since.

Mr. President, within the EU there are various cases of languages belonging to historical European peoples that, because they are not associated with a state, are not permitted to be used in the debates of this Parliament, and this constitutes a democratic and representational deficit.

One such case is that of the Basque language, Euskara, which is not a minority language, but an official language, at least in the southern part of the Basque Country, which administratively falls within the Spanish state.

But that is not the case, and please do not take it personally, Commissioner, with the Northern Basque Country, part of the French State, whose president, before the United Nations Assembly, stated that denying respect to national identities and languages is fostering humiliation, and that without such respect, there will be no peace in the world.
And then, neither Basque, nor Corsican, Breton, nor Occitan receive the slightest official recognition nor have support to respect or promote their use.

Therefore, I call on the Fundamental Rights Agency to monitor and work toward ensuring that within the Member States, the rights of the native language of any European people are not violated, nor are its inhabitants discriminated against, and that all such languages are recognized as official within their respective territories.

Madam President, first of all, I would like to thank our rapporteur, Claudio Fava, for his outstanding work in achieving a common position with the Council, which includes numerous improvements proposed by our House.
With this Directive, we once again demonstrate our commitment to building a common migration policy based on a comprehensive approach.
The approach taken by this Directive is clear: to combat mafias, punish unscrupulous employers, and protect exploited immigrants who have no form of social protection.
We want to eliminate the unjust low wages for immigrants that also distort average salaries, particularly in sectors such as construction, agriculture, domestic work, and hospitality.
These measures require great courage and political will, as there are numerous vested interests and substantial amounts of money involved in the underground economy.
Precisely in difficult times, it is more important than ever to manage migration flows intelligently, with generosity but also with responsibility.
The easy thing would be to succumb to the temptation of not attempting to address the underground economy.
We cannot turn a blind eye and leave about eight million irregular immigrants in a state of vulnerability, facing working conditions approaching slavery.

Mr. President, if we want this Directive to be effective, rigorous inspections are necessary, along with economic penalties and even criminal sanctions in the most serious cases, which will serve as a deterrent for business operators.
We will thus succeed in reducing the informal economy and eliminating the migration incentive created by the possibility of working irregularly.
It must be clear that the only option for working in Europe is through legal channels.

Therefore, we wish to continue with the global approach and request the Commission, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Baró, to present the new blue cards—quotation marks used for emphasis—as soon as possible for the other occupational categories.
Dear colleagues, Ms. Ferrero-Waldner, Mr. Solana, I would first like to thank the three speakers for their work, and as the shadow rapporteur on the report on the CFSP, I will refer to
specifically to this report, beginning by congratulating Mr. Saris Volski on the outcome of his work and the cooperation provided by the other groups, resulting in a consensual outcome.

Just as this Parliament has repeatedly called for the European Union to be equipped with the necessary instruments to speak with one voice in the world, the Chamber itself also knows how to remain cohesive in assessing and advancing the major priorities in common foreign and security policy.

The commitment of our group, the Socialist Group, has followed a dual direction: on one hand, introducing or reinforcing major issues that are central to us, running transversally through all foreign policy areas, such as the fight against climate change, the promotion of...

peace throughout the planet or a commitment to human development. On the other hand, propose elements for rebalancing the geographical priorities of the Common Foreign and Security Policy compared to how they originally appeared in the text, if at all, or introduce them as new elements if they were absent.

In this way, we have advocated, for example, for greater clarification of the relationships between the institutional frameworks and cooperation mechanisms involved in the recently defined Eastern dimension.

We have advocated for greater diversification and increased significance in our relations with Africa, a continent that we often only remember when particularly virulent conflicts arise, and sometimes not even then.

With regard to Latin America, we have wished to reflect the current processes of negotiation of association agreements—the first bilateral regional agreements ever concluded by the European Union in its entire history.

Regarding the Mediterranean, we have opposed a reductionist approach that speaks solely of security, opting instead to include the entire rich political, economic, and socio-cultural heritage contained within the Barcelona process.

Regarding the amendments for the Plenary, our Group has not submitted any, believing that in this way the balance achieved through the commitments is strengthened; therefore, we will oppose most of the amendments in order not to weaken the agreement reached in the Committee on Foreign Affairs.
Mr. President, Venezuela's referendum has ended in a victory for the "yes" vote, elegantly acknowledged by the democratic opposition.

It is no less true that there has been no equal opportunity in the campaign, and that the ruling party has enjoyed overwhelming support from the entire state apparatus, while the opposition has suffered continuous harassment and coercion.
Even under those conditions, the country has become practically divided into two halves, and it will be very difficult to build the future based solely on one of them.
The European Union must promote dialogue, inclusion, and consensus among Venezuelan political and social actors for the country's benefit.
It is not through visceral attitudes, dismissals, or insults that we will help Venezuela find its way toward a democratic, pluralistic, and free society.

In this regard, we criticize the Venezuelan Government's decision to expel a Spanish eurodeputy, and even more so the manner in which this expulsion was carried out, but we appeal to this House to avoid

that our representatives make statements during their visits to third countries that breach local legislation in force, and even less so that they insult a head of state, no matter how deserving of criticism he may be.
With these attitudes, we are jeopardizing future missions of the European Parliament to other countries.

Mr. President, I have just returned from Venezuela, where I was invited by its electoral authority to serve as a member of an international electoral observation group for the referendum held on the 15th.

The European members of that group have submitted a report to the National Electoral Council, offering a generally positive assessment of the process in terms of its organization, transparency, participation, free and secret exercise of the right to vote, and security throughout all its stages.

Regarding the statements made by Deputy Herrero on a Venezuelan television channel, he pointed out that they aimed to delegitimize the ongoing electoral process, level serious accusations against democratic institutions in that country, and border on interference in the internal affairs of a sovereign nation.
Our Parliament should not foster any kind of confrontation with the democratic institutions of Venezuela.
On the contrary, it would be incumbent upon the honorable member to provide explanations to this Chamber regarding an action that affects us all.

Madam President, at this late hour on Monday, the Commission likely has matters of greater concern than this report.
In short, what we are doing is simply responding to a report from the Commission, a report on the implementation of Regulation 1206/2001.
Council Recommendation of 28 May 2001 on cooperation between the judicial authorities of the Member States in the field of the taking of evidence in civil or commercial matters.

We are in an area that is genuinely non-communitarian, an intergovernmental one, in which, from the Council, through the Regulation, efforts have been made to encourage cooperation among those judicial bodies.
But by doing so, I believe there is a certain lack of rigor on the part of the European Union.

That is, certain measures have been foreseen, for example, the issuance of practical guidelines, the use of electronic media, etc., but I acknowledge that it is quite difficult for the Commission to take action.

I do not know what the Commission has achieved or intends to achieve, but it seems quite difficult to accomplish, given the considerable challenges posed by this form of intergovernmental cooperation.

In other words, we are asking judicial authorities to collaborate with one another—27 countries, judicial authorities handling 22 different languages, with very different legal systems, and we are striving to achieve results.
The Commission has noted that the results are not good, that there is significant delay in these cooperation mechanisms, and certainly that we lack the resources to do so.
The ideal would be to have material resources.
For example, what could the Commission do to provide judicial bodies, especially those at a lower level, with technological resources such as a videoconferencing system?
Videoconferencing may be a utopia for many small courts across the European Union, but it appears to be the only way to achieve it.
I believe the European Union should rely on existing institutions, specifically the European Judicial Network.
We would need to strengthen this institution so that judges themselves, through their cooperation systems, could carry out this type of communication.
On the other hand, any training for judges is essential.
This is something the Commission cannot do on its own either.
but the Commission can indeed encourage the development of this type of training courses in community law and in the knowledge of the various legal systems.
Then there is a major difficulty, which is the language problem, that is, how a Spanish judge might communicate with a Finnish judge, given their vastly different languages.
One cannot expect judges, in addition to having to manage their own legal system—which is already quite complex—to be required to possess knowledge of such matters.
What can we do from a practical standpoint to facilitate judges' tasks of interpretation and translation?
translation, that is, what could we do to help them with it?
in presenting this report, express no kind of rigor or severity toward the Commission.
I believe the work is quite difficult.
What I would like to know, taking advantage of the fact that we have here a commissioner who is interested in this issue, is what the Commission thinks could be done to further...
rigor in this 2001 regulation, somewhat imprecise, somewhat voluntarist, and I do not know whether, from a practical standpoint, it may have any consequences or whether progress can be made with it.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, I am very pleased to be able to conclude this legislative term with the final approval of the last of the maritime packages.

However, I recall that when I arrived at the European Parliament in 1999, we experienced a major maritime accident—the sinking of the vessel Erika—off the French coast, which gave rise to an initial set of legislative proposals aimed at preventing and addressing such accidents.
We thought we had already overcome the danger, but shortly afterward, the coasts of Galicia were devastated by tar from another accident even more severe than the first, that of the ship.

Since then, in the Transport Committee we have worked on successive legislative packages, striving to overcome the numerous resistances we encountered from shipowners, oil companies, classification societies, and also from certain Member States that rejected our proposals, considering them overly demanding.

Over these ten years, we have adopted various Directives and Regulations, some of which we have had to revise again because, due to cuts introduced by the Council, they proved ineffective in their initial formulation.

We have approved the rules for the introduction of double-hull vessels, the European Fund for Major Oil Spills, and safe refuge ports, and we are now reviewing and adopting the liability of maritime passenger transporters, the investigation of the

maritime accidents, the Community monitoring and information system for maritime traffic, control by the port State, and finally, the regulation and directive concerning ship inspection and classification organizations.
And all of this is good and desirable, and should enter into force and be applied by all member states as soon as possible.
For this reason, I feel satisfied.
But the legislative term ends, I leave Parliament, and I go away with the regret of not having succeeded in making the implementation of existing control systems mandatory.
They are patented to determine when and how much waste from bilges and fuel tanks has been illegally discharged into the sea by a specific vessel.
that is, a type of black box tachometers that can be inspected by maritime authorities each time a vessel arrives at port.

I believe we owe the marine environment a greater level of attention and stricter procedures to address the extensive pollution we generate, which, if left unaddressed, will impact our food chain and our lives, ultimately forcing us to pay a heavy price.
I trust that they will not have to wait another ten years to more effectively and efficiently control illegal marine discharges.

Mr. President, the revision of the Eurovignette Directive is a matter of great importance that concerns and troubles us all, particularly the peripheral countries, which feel themselves to be victims of this proposal.
The European Commission has decided to address the internalization of the external costs of road transport by attributing three new costs: atmospheric pollution, noise pollution, and congestion.

Although the principles underlying the proposal—those who use pay and those who pollute pay—are reasonable, the solution proposed is by no means reasonable, as it will be discriminatory toward a sector that has been suffering for months from the disastrous effects of the economic crisis.
Demonizing road freight transport, although not the intention, has ended up being the outcome.

This proposal, ladies and gentlemen, is ill-timed, and rather than achieving the expected goals of sustainable transport, it would amount to a death sentence for a large number of European small and medium-sized enterprises that employ thousands of people.

people in the European Union who daily contribute to the distribution of goods, ensuring products reach their final consumer, a consumer who will see their products become more expensive due to the implementation of these fees.

Ladies and gentlemen, we need a competitive, sustainable, and environmentally friendly transportation system, without overlooking the road, as it remains the only means of reaching all destinations at present.
Modal change remains a dream, far from becoming a reality.
Trans-European networks, maritime corridors, and cross-border connections remain, in some cases, projects rather than reality.
Ladies and gentlemen, when the titan of the global economy sinks, it is not possible to ask the orchestra to continue playing, let alone for joy.

Mr. President, on July 15, 2008, the European Commission adopted a communication addressed to the Council and the Parliament, recommending the establishment of a strategic partnership between the European Union and Mexico.
In turn, the European Council on Foreign Relations recognized Mexico as a strategic partner on October 13, pending the Parliament's opinion on the matter.
Let us recall that already at the seventh meeting of the Mixed Parliamentary Commission between the European Union and Mexico, held at the end of October last year, we stated that a.
This strategic partnership between Mexico and the European Union represents a necessary boost to strengthen and develop the true potential of our bilateral relations.
We highlighted the favorable state of relations between both parties and the positive assessment of the current Global Agreement.

We emphasize the need for our respective executives to strengthen political, economic, and cooperation collaboration, particularly in the face of the new challenges of all kinds arising from the financial and economic crisis shaking our continents.

With the great country that Mexico is, we share values and objectives such as the development of democratic forms of government, commitment to gender equity, consolidation of the rule of law, equitable and sustainable development, and respect for human rights.

We advocate strengthening cooperation in the fight against organized crime, terrorism, and drug trafficking, based on the principles of shared responsibility and strict adherence to international law.
Consequently, we will support the proposal for recommendations on the European Union-Mexico Strategic Association, which will be submitted to Parliament this morning.
With regard to the report on microcredit, I would like to congratulate all the rapporteurs, as they have done outstanding work.
have undoubtedly improved the Commission's initiative.

but that had not yet been transferred to the sector or the community context, and I believe this presents an opportunity to do so, particularly given the economic and financial context in which we currently find ourselves.

the European guarantee for microcredit loans as an instrument that can facilitate such access and has been included in the report.
And I conclude by thinking that, without a doubt, this will lay the foundations for us to create a harmonious framework within the European context that will drive forward the microcredit sector.

Madam President, taking advantage of the fact that we are discussing the budget, I understand that we must prepare ourselves for next year's negotiation regarding the continuation of coal subsidies, an indigenous energy source that is indispensable.

And I say this with the necessary advance notice, because, improperly, some high-ranking official, by misleading public opinion, felt free to express an opposing view, leaking it to an economic publication.
None of that.
We will need to continue supporting the coal from my country after 2012, and I want this recorded accordingly in the minutes.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, this question, as Mr. Boclan has explained, is particularly timely given the diplomatic conference on the ship recycling convention that will take place in May.
Therefore, I would like to congratulate Mr. Brok, not only for the question but also for the resolution he has prepared.

In the People's Party we support the fight against the poor practices and methods currently being used to dismantle ships, and therefore we back Mr. Brockland's resolution proposal. However, there are certain points that concern us.

The first is paragraph three, which he has also already pointed out, stating that ships in their entirety must be considered hazardous waste and, as a consequence, must fall within the scope of the Basel Convention.

We have submitted an amendment to this section because there are differing interpretations both within and outside the European Union, and we believe the Basel Convention must be taken into account and not contradicted, but it should be the future international agreement on ship recycling that defines the specific details of its implementation.
The draft resolution also requests, in its fourteenth point, a financing mechanism based on mandatory contributions from the maritime transport sector.

We find it rather premature at this stage to speak of mandatory contributions, given that the International Maritime Organization has not yet decided which system will be adopted for this financing, and I also believe the European Commission does not have a clear position on this matter.
Finally, in point fifteen, the Commission is urged to clearly establish that the State with jurisdiction over waste is the responsible State.
We also do not believe it is the Commission's competence to decide this, which should likewise be determined through an international agreement.

The key is to ensure coherence between the future ship recycling agreement and the Basel Convention on the transboundary movement of hazardous wastes and their disposal, taking into account not only the Basel Convention but also all existing legislation.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, colleagues, first of all, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, with whom we share significant agreement regarding this program, and to congratulate ourselves as well, because during a period of economic crisis, it is essential to maintain a program of this nature, one with clear positive social impacts.

Although we are facing a consultation procedure from the European Parliament, we must send a clear political signal that, at this time of economic uncertainty emanating from Brussels and Strasbourg, we are not exclusively preoccupied.

not only concerned with the recovery of the financial system, but we are fully aware that social policies, especially those supporting those who need it most—the poorest in the European Union—must also grow.

This food distribution program must continue to be regarded as part of the common agricultural policy; here we agree with the Commission, not only because European agriculture has a distinctly social character, because it is a functioning instrument, because it is more needed than ever at present, and because it must continue to operate.

From the Socialist Group, just like the rapporteur, we oppose this program being kept confidential between the European Union and the member states, as this would constitute discrimination among states according to their wealth, potentially resulting in the program being unimplementable in the countries most in need.

It is incomprehensible that now, precisely when it is most needed, the Commission seeks to save community funds in the most social aspect of the CAP, especially considering that there are always budget surpluses in the agricultural budget each year.
Full community funding is essential for us.
We need to ensure that this program reaches all countries, and above all, the most vulnerable ones.
Forty-three million potential beneficiaries of this program are calling on us to make greater efforts, and in no case to cut budgetary spending.
Austerity yes, but not at the expense of the poorest.
And it is precisely to ensure that it reaches citizens of all member states that the program must cover the entire journey of the food distributed.

Therefore, on behalf of the Socialist Group, for which I am responsible for the report, we have tabled an amendment to ensure that all expenses related to distribution— including transportation, storage, and administrative costs—are covered by community funds.
We also agree with the rapporteur that food should preferably be community-sourced and of high quality.
It is the position consistent with that upheld by this Parliament in other similar programs recently discussed in this Chamber, such as the school fruit distribution plan.
Nothing more, and thank you very much.
We hope that the Commission will take into account this Parliament's position when voting, and above all that the plan will be implemented in the European Union as soon as possible.
The Arctic region is one of the most fragile regions on our planet.
The consequences of unrestricted exploitation of its natural resources would be catastrophic, not only for the immediate environment and indigenous population, but for the world as a whole.

The melting of vast areas has brought these risks back into focus, making it necessary to establish a new global regulatory framework to protect the Arctic, similar to the one already in place for the Antarctic, while taking into account the differences that have already been highlighted in the debate.

An international treaty among all involved parties, undoubtedly including the European Union, is necessary to protect its unique environment, ensure the full sustainability of all types of human activities, and multilaterally regulate navigation through the new maritime routes becoming accessible.
The Arctic Council, during its several years of existence, has been a model of cooperation for managing common challenges.

At this time of difficulty and uncertainty, we must elevate that spirit and sense of harmony to higher levels, in order to prevent the states of the region or other international actors from becoming entangled in geostrategic rivalries, thereby forgetting what must remain our common goal: the preservation of a vast shared heritage.

Mr. President, the information you provide me is correct, but the impression we European parliamentarians, and ultimately European citizens, have is that we face a very dangerous gap in community law.
At this moment, as fiscal demands continue to intensify across each of the member states, mobility is nearly impossible.
And when we asked the Commission, the reason for the pragmatic approach is that the Commission never finds support in the Council.

We are caught in a vicious cycle whereby the Commission refers us to the Council and the Council refers us back to the Commission. Yet meanwhile, the reality is that citizens living in this Europe we wish to build cannot move between different countries or establish relationships due to the heavy tax burden resulting from the lack of fiscal harmonization.
fiscal. Can the Council do something to help us break out of this vicious cycle?
Madam President, I am pleased for once that the regular member is not present, that Mr. Tajani has come instead, because Mr. Tajani has the advantage of having been a European Parliament member.
And as a European parliamentarian, I know that you have experienced the frustration we European parliamentarians feel regarding the implementation of Community law.

Now, in Parliament we tend to interfere with the Commission. I believe we are asking the Commission to perform an impossible task, because all Community law and the implementation of Community law are based on indirect application.

That is, the Commission has only a few officials at its headquarters, where it receives some complaints and has limited capacity for action. But at this moment, furthermore, the trend is to restrict budgetary powers, meaning the Commission will no longer have the ability to do so.
All Community law, and the implementation of Community law, is based on the actions of national authorities, national parliaments, national judges, and national officials.
And here, in this respect, I do not believe we can expect too much from the Commission.
What we need to do is assist the Commission, and I believe that in Mrs. Frassoni's report there are certain points that could be helpful in achieving this implementation.
I am referring to those relating to the correlation of directives, the collaboration of national parliaments, and the actions of national courts.
Mr. President, two years ago, during a mission led by my colleague from the group present here, Marián, I had the opportunity to visit.
Sierra Leone and to be present at this Tribunal and to be aware of the immense work that this Tribunal was carrying out, not only for Sierra Leone but for all humanity.
The Special Court for Sierra Leone, as has already been stated here, has set a precedent.

A precedent, as the Resolution rightly points out, because it is the first international tribunal funded by voluntary contributions, the first tribunal established in the country where the alleged crimes took place, and also the first, as already noted, to prosecute a former prime minister.

For all these reasons, not only because it sets a precedent but also because it serves as a reference for other tribunals established ad hoc, such as those in Rwanda, the former Yugoslavia, Cambodia, or Lebanon, we consider it essential that this resolution be adopted, to which we have been jointly working with the Tribunal for several months.
Two years ago we adopted a resolution to support its funding. Two years ago the Court was going through a difficult period during which it was running out of...
money and did not have sufficient support. Here, we must also thank the European Commission for providing, so to speak, financial backing for this Court.

Now we are primarily asking for two things: first, that those convicted serve their sentences. What is at stake is no longer merely the functioning of the Tribunal, which will conclude next year, but the legacy that this Tribunal will leave behind. Second, obviously, all of this must be accompanied by increased funding.
In short, the Special Court of Sierra Leone is an example and a reference for all of us and for all courts that have judged war crimes.

It is an example and a reference, and it is a lesson that the second poorest country on this planet is teaching us, where, upon entering the courthouse, we saw a motto written: Without justice, there is no peace.
And precisely for this reason, we have a moral obligation, not only as Europeans but as human beings, that the legacy of this Court remain permanent in history.

I was just one minute short of telling the lady Commissioner how much I value my friendship and affection for her, and indeed, my friendship and affection for Mr. Orban.

Mr. President, I believe the regulatory proposals submitted to us by the Commission are important and necessary, and on the other hand, it was also important and necessary that, from the
The European Parliament insisted on the principles emphasized by the two rapporteurs of the European Parliament, Mr. Svieska and Mr. Depré, and these are the principles of community competence.

This is a matter of community competence, in which, for practical reasons, it is advisable to maintain certain competences for the Member States, but, as Mr. Barró has stated, limited in scope and duration.
The Community competence is not available for renunciation, and neither the Council, nor the Commission, nor the Parliament has the authority to waive such Community competences.

Having established this, it is an exceptional procedure. I believe the amendments we have discussed and submitted, which I think will be approved by Parliament tomorrow, will allow the adoption of this package of measures in first reading.

I also hope that, from now on and building upon this foundation, we can make progress in developing a genuine European private law, which is becoming increasingly necessary, as Mr. Sogor has highlighted, for example, in his most recent intervention.

In short, Mr. President, I would like to congratulate my colleagues, Mr. Debrey and Mr. Svieska, and thank the Commission and the Council for their willingness to cooperate with us on this matter.

Madam President, Commissioner, I have signed this resolution and requested to speak on this matter because I was a witness to what was called an expulsion, but in reality was the kidnapping of our colleague Luis Herrero.

Moreover, I was able to confirm that in Venezuela it is common practice to engage in political persecution, abuse of power by the Chavista regime, intimidation by the aggressor, threats, disregard for human dignity, and the misuse of justice, among other things.
The case of Manuel Chávez is the final drop that has filled the cup and led to this resolution, but there are thousands of cases equally egregious, cited in the resolution and others that are not.
as in the case of Ligio Cedeño, who was born in the modest Caracas neighborhood of Petare, well known for its danger and also because he voted for a mayor who was not from the Chavista regime.

Eligio studied thanks to the help of others, and specifically with support from Citibank, he managed to establish his own business, Banco de Caracas, and furthermore lived normally, helping those in greatest need.
Today, however, she is imprisoned in an illegal Caracas jail, after being detained for two years without any coherent charges.
Its only crime is being part of the economic oligarchy.
Another case is Nilson Moreno, a student leader at the Universidad de los Andes, repeatedly elected to the University Council and president of the Federation of University Centers.
In 2003, he won the election for the current Ministry of the Interior and Justice, and this is his crime.
He is currently charged with murder in the attempt, violent lascivious acts, and all this despite having been cleared of previous allegations.

Situations like yours are the daily reality in this country, where persecuting opponents with the intent of excluding them from political life and discouraging dissent have become commonplace occurrences.

However, from here it is necessary to send a message of hope to Venezuelan democracy. Despite the difficulties, I am confident that democracy will prevail and that President Chávez will be removed from office through the ballot box.

Madam President, Commissioner, it is indeed undeniable that progress has been made in recent times; however, despite all that has been achieved, the south of the Union continues to burn, and it burns more intensely each day.
Therefore, the resolution adopted by this Parliament cannot be just another resolution intended merely to highlight our profound concern over the misfortunes of others.
Forest fires occur due to one cause or another, but no one can deny that they are fueled by climate change.

And, regardless of any responsibilities that might exist in certain states or autonomous communities, the fact remains that this Parliament must clearly urge the Commission to act swiftly and to mobilize all the resources at its disposal—resources that are by no means insignificant.
Neither the victims, nor the damages suffered, nor the needs for reforestation understand bureaucracy, nor can they accommodate budgetary rhythms.

Priority must be established and action must be taken, and in this case, acting means adjusting strategies, mobilizing resources, increasing them, and optimally coordinating preventive policies, demanding, if necessary,

case, responsibilities to states that lack them, but assuming that this is not a problem confined to some Southern European states, and that climate change does not affect only certain states—it affects all and is a shared concern.
Therefore, one of the most immediate tasks facing this Parliament must be to ensure that the European Union secures clear commitments at the upcoming Copenhagen Summit.

robust measures to minimize greenhouse gas emissions as much as possible, because they are evidently at the root of fires, their causes, and these, in turn, are among the causes of further climate change.
Therefore, adopting this Resolution is now undoubtedly the challenge facing this Parliament.
But that challenge set by the Commission will be met only if, truly, Mr. Commissioner, you wish to prevent the south of the Union, which does exist, from continuing to burn.
Mr. President, thank you very much for granting me the floor. I am addressing Mr. Barroso.

Mr. President, I would like, first of all, to congratulate Mrs. McGuinness on this initiative, which highlights the significant role played by the CAP in addressing international food supply needs.

Now that the European Commission appears intent on reducing the share of the CAP within the community budget, it must be stated very loudly and very clearly that the CAP is no longer a priority, but food sufficiency should be, and past decades have shown that without the CAP, food sufficiency is very difficult, if not impossible, to achieve.
Agriculture cannot therefore be compared to other sectors of the economy capable of thriving in a free market, because the food market is not a free market.

The farmer needs the support of the European Union in order to sustain his business, and in turn, the Union needs the farmer to maintain an agricultural model capable of supplying sufficient, high-quality food to an increasingly demanding citizenry.
I therefore believe that the CAP would need to undergo a change in direction, but not disappear.

To achieve this, it would be necessary to ensure direct support for farmers, as well as to restore a policy for managing agricultural markets that provides greater price stability, benefiting not only farmers but also consumers and third countries.

It would be necessary to establish a framework of best practices to foster balanced relationships among the various actors in the food chain, avoiding abusive practices and promoting a fairer distribution of commercial margins.

Moreover, there is a need for a consumer information policy targeting European consumers that emphasizes the efforts made by community producers to comply with European Union environmental regulations.

According to a United Nations report, in 2009 approximately 2,700 people became victims of human trafficking in the European Union.

With these figures in hand, it is essential that the European Union's action be primarily based on the protection of victims, with particular attention to the situation of women and children, who are the most vulnerable.
We cannot continue to remain indifferent spectators to this theater, this spectacle of sexual exploitation unfolding before our eyes.

That is why I fully support this Parliament's request that assistance to victims be unconditional, more robust, and accompanied by harsher penalties, as the honorable Mr. Barrott has just indicated to us.
That said, I would like to request the President and the Council to make full use of all European legislation within their reach—existing and future—to protect victims of human trafficking.

I believe that the proposal for a European protection order for victims, which I personally requested from the Spanish Presidency during the debates on the Stockholm Programme and which has ultimately come to light, will be an effective tool in combating these crimes.

I expect a firm commitment, as we have already seen, from the Spanish Presidency for this directive to provide victims with effective, special protection measures throughout the entire territory of the European Union.

I hope that those of us who bear the responsibility of addressing society's most serious problems provide a genuine and concrete response to this grave issue of human trafficking, and that we do not confine ourselves merely to good words.
We owe this truly to all the victims.

It has already been a long time since Fidel Castro and his oligarchy dismantled the ideals of the Cuban Revolution, which once resonated so powerfully and inspired such hope among peoples throughout the world, particularly among the most promising youth internationally.
What characterizes, however, the dictatorship of the Castro brothers at this moment is their impiety toward political and conscience prisoners, their impiety toward their own people.
It reminds me of Franco on his deathbed, with the mistreatment of political prisoners that he likewise regarded as commonplace, and his identical obsessive disdain toward exiles.
Castro ends up like Franco, and like all enemies of freedom who entrench themselves in power.

Mr. Orlando Zapata Tamayo, a 42-year-old Afro-descendant bricklayer who only demanded improved prison conditions, died after 86 days on hunger strike and seven years in prison for advocating respect for human rights.

During these six months of Spanish presidency, I would like to distinguish, on one hand, between the political leadership of the presidency and, on the other, the work carried out by the officials who have been operating behind the scenes.
It is the first one that has failed, and this is due to the lack of proper leadership from the Government Presidency, which has instead been shaped more by political swings.
However, I wish to highlight the efforts of the officials, their valuable commitment to serving Europe and Spain.
And the truth is, Mr. President, Spain has outstanding professional civil servants, and if there is anything to regret, it is that in everyday reality they are too often marginalized.
That said, it is appropriate to acknowledge that some commendable initiatives have been undertaken during these six months.
Among them, I am pleased to mention the organ donation and transplantation plan, and the treatment afforded to patients' rights in cross-border healthcare.
Even more uncertain have become the agreements regarding the new 2020 Strategy, and especially those concerning the European External Action Service, both of which remain too fragile at this stage.
Regrettably, what has occurred with the European Protection Order for Victims of Gender-Based Violence is deeply concerning, and the same dismal outcome has been produced by the
a basic European patent for the lives of thousands of businesses, partially blocked by linguistic issues encouraged by Spanish legislation.

However, we must agree that being unable to advance their policy regarding Cuba has been highly beneficial for the Cubans.
Finally, the progress made toward better economic governance in Europe cannot in any way be attributed to her presidency, conspicuously absent on the most relevant issues.
Goodbye, then, to the Spanish presidency.

A positive conclusion I do want to draw from this experience is my fantasy as a committed Europeanist being excited at the thought that it might signal the end of rotating presidencies, as they have been conceived until now.

The tactics of closing deals hastily at any price have not enhanced the image of Spanish management, which has been described as clumsy in its approach to achieving any respectable goal.
Spain is a great nation and will remain so, despite poor management during this period.
Our diplomats and officials have remained steadfast, and thanks to this, Spain's prestige will endure.
The truth is that the evaluation of this presidency will weigh more on Spain's situation than on what was or was not accomplished here.

Mr. Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, in the coming months we will fully engage in the debate on the new CAP.

It is important to reach a good agreement, so I hope the Belgian Presidency will achieve what the Spanish Presidency did not: steer the discussions in such a way that member states commit to maintaining, in the future, a policy

a truly strong common agricultural policy, with a sufficient budget to meet the needs of the twenty-seven Member States, both new and old, so that we do not face the same situation as in 2005, when funds were insufficient and we had to invent modulation.

In the upcoming reform of the CAP, we are betting on the viability of a sector that not only provides intangible goods and services such as environmental stewardship, but which should also ensure the supply of food to European citizens.

Madam President, Commissioner, colleagues, I agree with Commissioner Cholos that this report is necessary, indeed very necessary, and arrives at an opportune moment, as the abuses committed by large-scale distribution have been demonstrated, severely disrupting the various links in the food chain.

The Bové report, which at times may go too far in attempting to be overly interventionist, must be acknowledged—viewed from the standpoint of the social market economy that we advocate as the European People's Party—to contain responses to the legitimate demands of European producers.

Moreover, it brings transparency to the various parts of the food chain, which is always beneficial, and provides significantly greater opportunities and support for the weaker sectors within that chain, while also clearly supporting European consumers in gaining better knowledge of the chain.

The European food industry could perhaps have been given greater consideration in this report, and that would have been very beneficial; however, this report also includes measures aimed at curbing price volatility, which was very necessary.

Measures are also being taken to ensure equity within these links of the chain, as requested by the Environmental Commission, and there is also a call to combat abusive and unfair practices by large retailers.
It is high time to establish a list of abusive practices and to prohibit them unequivocally through European legislation.
It is also important to implement measures to prevent difficult practices associated with private-label products that undermine competition and violate intellectual property laws.

For all these reasons, we must support the Bové report and all the colleagues from the different parliamentary committees—INCO, ENVI, or Agriculture—who have worked diligently to ensure this report finally sees the light of day and helps European producers move forward.

The European Union is today a leader in the fight against climate change and has been for some time, pioneering measures and conservation efforts.

However, as demonstrated by the unmet objectives, it appears that willpower was lacking—not only in implementation and coordination, which have also been mentioned here, but also in expansion, to go beyond our extremely limited special conservation areas.
Certainly, biodiversity is not a museum piece that can be confined to a ghettoized area, as sometimes seems to happen, occasionally glancing at it now and then.
Biodiversity lives and knows no borders; it spans the entire Earth in a domino effect, where if one piece falls, all the others fall as well.

And I draw attention to the need for a more global and communal will, particularly with regard to land, because subsidiarity, as the sole method for managing land or forests, may become one of the new formidable enemies of biodiversity.

That is why I draw your attention to paragraph 67 and the possible vote that may follow, and I ask you to reject the second part, as I am convinced that a common policy on land would contribute to the conservation of the entire territory.

so varied, so diverse for better and for worse, and yet so lacking in political will among the states, and so increasingly in need of a Europe that is ever more united, in the full sense of the word, and what that implies—greater unity also for the land.

The objective of this European Union pharmacovigilance system is to strengthen the monitoring, transparency, and communication of the safety of marketed medicines, particularly with regard to their risk profiles and any adverse reactions that may occur.

It is important to highlight the establishment of the European Pharmacovigilance Committee, which will assess and issue recommendations to the European Medicines Agency regarding safety issues that may arise with medicinal products marketed in the European Union.

It is very important that the solution reached regarding the reporting of suspected adverse reactions to EudraVigilance, by both Member States and marketing authorization holders, has been established.

EudraVigilance will be the sole source of information, but at the same time, the competent authority of the Member State where the adverse reaction is suspected to have occurred will also be informed, and this is very important.
It should also be emphasized that consumers now have the opportunity to report any adverse reaction, and in this regard, the establishment and management by the Agency of a dedicated portal.
The European portal on medicine safety, which in turn links to the portals of each Member State, will help improve citizens' understanding of the safety of the medicines they consume.
And finally, we cannot overlook the fact that, since pharmacovigilance plays a fundamental role in protecting public health, the approval of the Directive and the
This directive and regulation will significantly contribute to that goal by improving not only current procedures but also the coordination of actions among the various member states.

for greater, more, and better employment, quality supplies, international cooperation that prevents piracy, and the promotion of governance of our seas, which know no boundaries and should avoid sustainability issues, as in the case of Iceland.

Cairo. How long will the complicit silence of Western democracies last?

terrorist. Why is the motive not cited? The condition of the Christians, which is the unmistakable cause of the massacre.

To this end, it is very important that emissions be widely disseminated, since cities produce nearly 80 percent of greenhouse gases.
Today, there is no such dissemination, nor, worse still, a common standard for measuring these emissions themselves.
There is a non-profit organization called the CO2 Outreach Project, which already hosts information from thousands of companies and which I believe could also be used by municipalities.
Without their cooperation, without the cooperation of these municipalities and mayors, environmental action amounts to smoke—the very smoke we seek to escape.

Mr. President, some have argued that the condemnation of Morocco is too lenient, given the scale of human rights repression in the Sahara, where Morocco is nothing more than an occupying country without sovereignty or any legal title.

Mr. President, Commissioner, the Directive adopted in 2009 was a success because it updates the regulatory framework that has been governing toy safety.
It is a significant step in the right direction because it points us toward the path to follow, but it is not enough.
We must always remain vigilant and attentive to ensure that toys marketed do not endanger children's safety and health.
All toys available on the market must comply with current legislation.
We must review and improve surveillance mechanisms to detect hazardous toys, whether due to substances classified as toxic ECMR, fragrances, or other hazardous substances such as HPA compounds.
It is necessary that the guidelines to be followed be clearer regarding the minimum age warning and the dangers associated with improper use.
It is essential to ensure that the recommended age for toys is based on studies of children's behavior.
We must be able to adapt our regulations to scientific advancements, supported by robust studies, and become more rigorous in monitoring the market and the importation of toys.
It is necessary to work together and strengthen cooperation among Member States, as well as ensure joint efforts by customs authorities to monitor the supply chain of imported toys.
Citizens are preparing to make their holiday purchases, and we must assure them that the products they buy meet all safety requirements.
I believe that in this debate we have clearly seen that we all agree on supporting the objective of this report.

I repeat, what fears are there that something as necessary and straightforward as ensuring equal protection for victims throughout Europe will ultimately become a reality?
Having hoped that this Parliament will overwhelmingly approve this report, I address the Council and the Commission, reminding them that we have

We do not need to exclude one another, but rather support each other, by adopting this more concrete instrument today, and tomorrow the broader package of measures for victims that the Commission will present, to which I commit myself to contributing.

Madam President, the Group of the European People's Party will enthusiastically support this report, and my first congratulations go to the rapporteurs, the Commission, and the Council for this agreement.
We coined, Your Lordships, the term "democratic deficit" in the 1980s.
With it, we wanted to convey that in the European Union, decisions were made solely by the Council of Ministers upon proposal from the Commission, and this Parliament was consulted only.
The battle of this Parliament since the 1980s, through the Maastricht Treaty, the Treaty of Amsterdam, the Treaty of Nice, the Constitutional Treaty, and the Treaty of Lisbon, has been

bridge that democratic deficit. And I believe that with the Treaty of Lisbon, with the new powers granted to the European Parliament, and with the involvement of the national parliaments, we have achieved that goal.

And today we conclude this process with this citizen legislative initiative, which, in the work of the Convention, resulted from the constructive cooperation between national parliaments and the European Parliament.

I would like to recall here the names of Jürgen Mayer, our German Social Democratic colleague, and Alain Lamassur, who were the ones who brought that initiative to the Convention's Plenary and ensured its approval.
Therefore, I believe we have completed the circle, Madam President.
I believe that today, when we vote on this initiative, we will have brought forth, placed on the table, the death certificate of the concept of democratic deficit.
It is now our turn, Your Lordships, to act responsibly, and today we also have the opportunity to do so.
By voting on the Union's budget, we will fulfill what is expected of us: a responsible Parliament and a co-decision-making Parliament.
I believe it is a good day for Europe, ladies and gentlemen.

Mr. President, in January 2012, four hundred Spanish egg producers could disappear, approximately thirty percent of the total number in my country, resulting in the loss of three hundred metric tons of egg production.

And the European Union could cease producing those eighty million eggs, those two million tons, and we would merely end up having them covered by imports from third countries whose animal welfare standards are significantly lower than those of the European Union if we do not act with great intelligence.
This 1999 Directive, which requires increasing the space in cages for laying hens, obliges us to act with intelligence and a possible-minded vision, because otherwise.

We will only succeed in weakening European production and creating additional commercial opportunities for third countries, where space per bird is infinitely worse than the current conditions we have in the European Union.

It is a directive that requires enormous efforts from European producers. In Spain alone, we can estimate the cost increase at around six hundred million euros, but we must also remember that it has an economic impact extending to the egg-product industry and the food industry as a whole.

I therefore request the support of Your Lordships for the Popular Group's amendment to the second paragraph, which calls for seeking a solution, at the very least, for those responsible farming operations run by responsible entrepreneurs who are currently undergoing transformation and will be nearly completed by January 2012.

They must be supported in transforming those facilities and given time to complete the process, thus avoiding irreparable harm to farms and preventing an abrupt shortage in the community market, which would consequently lead to higher prices for consumers.
It is a matter of respecting the Directive, of giving the egg and egg product industry an opportunity, while also respecting animal welfare rights and consumers' right to reasonable prices.

and to establish a new direction that enables us to lay the foundations ensuring citizens' security and quality they choose at any given moment, especially since it also allows for clear regulation of the role of states, both those of origin that must make the payment and those receiving treatment.

Mr. President, today, moreover, is also European Day against Sexual Health.
Ladies and gentlemen, the data are concerning. The World Health Organization estimates that over three hundred million people contract a sexually transmitted disease involuntarily each year.

In Europe, as the European Forum on Sexual Health warns, it is concerning that the number of unwanted pregnancies among adolescents is increasing due to an excessive lack of education and information among our young people.

Indeed, as the Commission points out, there are various and diverse inequalities across Europe in access to sexual health services, depending on the country, and within these countries, depending on citizens' income levels.
To combat all of this, a European strategy is required.
Only in five member states are such plans in place, but in all of them, the number of unwanted pregnancies, sexually transmitted infections, and abortions has been reduced.
A common strategy that promotes prevention through the educational sector, expands information policies, increases efforts in family planning, and improves access to contraceptive methods.
a comprehensive and ambitious strategy, free of taboos, to ensure every citizen's rights to sexual health under the safest and most hygienic conditions.

an attack or a massive influx of immigrants, such as the one currently taking place in Lampedusa, where the exodus of Tunisian citizens toward the Italian coast could lead to a chronicle of a death foretold.

Madam President, it has already been stated that the backbone of pension systems in Europe is and must remain the public system, because it is the only system that ensures cohesion and, furthermore, reduces the risk of poverty in our society.

It remains striking that in OECD countries, the highest levels of income equity among citizens are found in the area of pensions, or that, due to the crisis, private pensions lost only 20% of their value in 2008, and yet still remain today far from the solvency levels required.

All of this demonstrates that, at a time of unavoidable reforms, the sustainability of our pension system must be addressed in a comprehensive manner, taking into account, among other factors, ambitious policies on fertility or work-life balance.

also ambitious policies regarding access to and retention in employment, or even immigration policies based on integration, which would ensure, from a demographic standpoint, the sustainability of our pension systems.

In my view, this is essential for an entire new generation of workers who, if being asked to contribute more in order to secure a decent pension, should likewise be required to make fewer sacrifices in order to access decent work.

Mr. President, Commissioner, we are at a crucial moment in shaping the future cohesion policy, which, following the entry into force of the Lisbon Treaty, focuses not only on economic and social aspects but also on territorial dimensions.
Territorial cohesion aims at achieving harmonious development among territories, so that their citizens can fully benefit from their respective territorial particularities.
However, to achieve this objective, it is also necessary to advance cooperation among the regions of the Union that face the same challenges and problems.

This is the objective pursued by the European Union's Strategy for the Atlantic region: a shared perspective to address common challenges faced by the countries in the region, including marine research, maritime surveillance, and environmental and economic challenges.

To achieve our objectives, it is necessary to adopt an integrated strategic approach aligned with the 2020 Strategy and the European Union's policies for the period following 2013, particularly regional and maritime policies.
However, Ladies and Gentlemen, above all, this strategy must be based on renewed multi-level governance, founded on closer involvement of public authorities
regional and local levels, Member States, the European Union, and stakeholders from the private sector and civil society organizations, including interregional organizations and networks.
It is a highly significant day to discuss equality between men and women, as we celebrate Women's Day, honoring those who a century ago fought for labor and economic rights and for women's suffrage.
We have made great progress over these hundred years, but much remains to be done, and true equality is still a dream, while the discrimination against women is a real fact.

I believe each of us, men and women alike, can do much more to achieve the true emancipation of women, because there are many women around the world whose right to live is threatened, whose dignity is violated, and whose freedom is denied.
Allow me to, on this International Women's Day, express my support and hope for all the women in the North African Revolution who are fighting for their democratic rights.
Because a woman cannot be worth half a man, neither in Arab countries nor anywhere else in the world.
Therefore, we are worth the same and deserve the same.
And I will continue fighting to achieve it, and as long as that equality does not exist, the Women's Day will continue to exist.
Although many may not want to see it, it is a reality that there are women who earn half as much as men, and that domestic violence primarily affects women and children.
And I repeat, we must continue denouncing all such incidents in this Parliament, and certainly I promise that I will continue working.
Mr. President, I wish to express my agreement with the views of the Members who have spoken here this morning and who emphasize respect for Parliament and the Commission.

Eurobarometer indicates that 79% of traders believe full harmonization in this area will have little or no effect on their activities abroad.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, allow me first of all to congratulate the rapporteur, Mr. Schwab, and Mrs. Gerhardt, on the excellent work they have accomplished.

Because if we wish to establish and pursue a genuine internal market for business-to-consumer relationships, we must

This balance entails agreeing on a minimum level of harmonization and allowing Member States the possibility to maintain or adopt additional regulations that enhance consumer protection.

I welcome the improvement in negotiations within the European Parliament, but I would like to emphasize that existing rights already established must not be reduced.

Mr. President, many experts are telling us that the next crisis may be an energy crisis, and that either we are not doing the necessary work to prevent it, or we are not doing it quickly enough to avoid it.
This coming Saturday, the World Forum for Nature calls on all of us to make a small gesture in the pursuit of sustainability.
Many citizens will turn off the lights for one hour, and many public administrations will follow suit.
but it is not enough.
Europe cannot continue relying on energies that are not its own, that are located far away, or on insecure energy sources.
We must help states build a new energy model in which renewable energies are the core of the new system.
Moreover, we must promote awareness of new consumption guidelines, because renewable energy and efficiency are undoubtedly the keys to future sustainability.

Finally, I would like to appeal to all countries of the Union to join this call, this willingness of five of them, including Spain, to increase CO₂ emission reductions to thirty percent, thereby demonstrating that Europe, in addition to being a leader in freedom and rights, is also a leader in its commitment to planetary sustainability.
Madam President, the majority of times I have spoken in this Parliament, I have done so to denounce violence against women, and once again I am here to combat it.

I must say that not only do I not grow weary of it, but I consider it essential to continue speaking publicly about this violation of human rights, as no cause can be effectively championed without a platform, and this Parliament is the best one available.

a speaker I have at my disposal to defend all those women who suffer abuse daily and who need us, legislators, to compel states to punish offenders according to the severity of the crime, and also to assist the victims.

I believe it is necessary to continue emphasizing, again and again, the protection of victims, as we already did in the European Protection Order for Victims, which I chaired and which I hope will become a reality as soon as possible.

families, children, elderly individuals, women, and men—should not be exploited for political opportunism, as advancing the solution to this social scourge honors us all.
The first step is to stop remaining silent, and the second is to take action, since although we have been discussing gender-based violence for decades, we have failed to reduce the number of deaths.

We must be doing something wrong, and therefore it is time to abandon demagogy and move from words to actions, providing the necessary support and response so that women can live with dignity and security.

a terrible enemy that tragically ends the lives of so many innocents. I say enough, enough already, and I ask all of you to say it with me.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, I agree with you that we must listen to citizens and restore confidence in the European project.

Because the adoption of these reports not only advances our progress toward revitalizing the single market—the key element in confronting the current economic crisis—but also responds to citizens' demands for further European integration and the strengthening of social rights.
Europe must respond to new global challenges and ensure that the European economy becomes a highly competitive social market economy that generates balanced growth with more and better employment.

However, this objective must be based on incorporating a social clause into all legislation related to the internal market, placing citizens at the center of our attention and priority, and strengthening their social rights.
