Mr. President, the blue tongue disease has been a constant threat to our livestock and, with it, also to our public health.

a disease against which we were fighting through the use of an activated vaccine, whose application entails the creation of restricted zones for animal movement, because the use of these vaccines may cause unintended viral spread in uninfected animals.

For these reasons, vaccination was restricted to those areas where infected animals had previously been found, thereby creating a double disadvantage for livestock farmers: on one hand, the disease itself, and on the other hand, the restrictions imposed.

However, all these problems appear to be avoidable through the new inactivated vaccine, which, in addition to preventing disruptions that may arise in the domestic livestock market, enables the development of new and broader prevention strategies—what is truly important—in the fight against this disease.
For all these reasons, it is of vital importance for this sector that we amend the Directive to allow its use, and that we do so as soon as possible.

Mr. President, the fulfillment of what our Treaties provide, particularly regarding the correspondence tables, cannot serve as an excuse for this regulation not coming into force before summer, as delaying it would only harm the livestock farmers.

Mr. President, first of all, I would like to thank the Committee on Petitions for the work it has carried out following the complaint submitted by FEPEX, the Spanish Organization of Fruit and Vegetable Producers and Exporters, based on the report published in 2007 by OLAF, the European Union's Anti-Fraud Agency.

I believe the conclusions of that report are very clear and fully justify the fact that the European Commission should have taken action long ago to clarify the functioning of the entry price system, at the very least in the tomato sector.

However, the Commission has not only failed to intervene to address potential loopholes created by the current Entry Price Regulation, but has also negotiated a new Agricultural Agreement with Morocco without first resolving the flaws in the existing import regime.

Madam President, it is high time that the European Commission took its responsibilities seriously and began investigating where frauds occur, and eventually demanded the recovery of unpaid customs duties.
I believe that no new agreement with Morocco should be ratified under any circumstances without the details we have outlined having been resolved.

Our objective is not to halt the signing of international agreements, but rather to ensure that such agreements do not seriously harm the interests of European productions and that the rules established in these agreements are respected.

With regard to the new agreement with Morocco, the European fruit and vegetable sector is requesting guarantees that the agreed measures will be effectively implemented, and this is only possible through the reform of the existing import pricing regime.
Until we have a new entry pricing system, we will not be able to reach a new agreement with Morocco.
We will not be able to ratify that agreement which the Commission has already finalized.

Moreover, if it is the responsibility of the Member States to carry out this control, they would be required to make a significant economic effort; however, it is also true that we have been calling for a long time for a European customs border system, similar to the one the United States has, in order to ensure effective control.

Mr. President, three observations regarding the situation surrounding the cucumbers.
I am the grandson of a German and I hold great affection for that country, but it is evident that the German authorities have acted in this case with haste and imprudence.

On the other hand, if we analyze the European response, we observe that the actions taken by the Food Safety Authority and the Commission have been muted, very subdued, and imprecise; they have failed to coordinate or lead effectively.
Third, the affected parties have the right to receive economic compensation promptly.

The involved Member States have demonstrated slowness, inefficacy, and ineffectiveness, and even now, after twenty-three deaths, we still do not know where the problem originates.

instability, unemployment, helplessness, and, of course, a health crisis with extremely high risks to public health. Because while you, from the Commission, echoed irresponsible statements by regional authorities

those two hundred million euros to repair, as much as possible, the damage caused.
To this end, it is necessary to find a legal framework that allows it—a legal framework that has already been found on other occasions, such as with mad cows or dioxins, and that should be found now.

German authorities, moreover, given the irresponsible allegations made, should undoubtedly support this economic solution from the outset and also finance, with their own funds, their communication campaigns aimed at restoring consumer trust lost in the production of fresh produce.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, nothing we do here or in the European Union will bring back the dead, and may what is being done now prevent further loss of life and break the chain of suffering among those who are ill through no fault of their own.

We do not know the causes, surely, but we do know certain things that we must name as they are. There has been negligence in political actions, and there has been negligence in the German federal government. It cannot act as if Hamburg were a city-state on another continent.

The arrogance displayed by Frau Merkel and her Minister of Health in frequently addressing other countries, if translated into diligent and effective coordination, would have prevented some of the events that have occurred.

And that negligence has had criminal consequences for a state that has been the Spanish state and for its most important sector, agriculture, leading to the ruin of thousands of families and small entrepreneurs, and damaging the economy for many years.
I ask you to go to the Council and request full compensation for those affected, since we cannot ask for lives for the dead.
and that control systems be reviewed.
and that responsibility be demanded from Mrs. Merkel.
and that less arrogance and more diligence be requested of him.
and that he be held responsible for the damages he has caused.

A bit more humility, a bit more sound management, and a bit less conservatism would have gone a long way in preventing this crisis from getting out of hand, sparing Spain and other southern farmers from enduring its painful consequences.
Please review the controls, please review the alert systems, and demand accountability, because in Spain we are calling on you, Commissioner, on the Council, and on the German State to do so.
Mr. President, we are facing a public health issue, an epidemic, as the Commissioner has said, and in response to this, someone has been sought to blame, if not the actual cause.

The system has failed. We have twenty-two dead, one hundred sixty affected, and three weeks later we still do not know the cause. And, appealing to a precautionary principle without scientific basis—I repeat, without scientific basis—Spain is being blamed.
Honorable deputies, the Spanish cucumbers were not contaminated.
contaminated, they were not contaminated. I repeat this because some conflicting versions have been heard here.
But to help you understand the significance of what these words have provoked, three hundred jobs could be affected by a loss of confidence.
An epidemic knows no borders.

I agree with you, Commissioner; you do not recognize borders. We are demanding responsibility for what has not been done, for what we must do immediately, because Germany currently faces a risk situation, with a higher number of affected individuals, but Spain is not to blame.
We are requesting compensation.
What is important and what unites us here today are the victims, because no one should feel as unprotected as European consumers feel today.
And it is out of respect for the victims that we need clarity and accountability.

And it is my responsibility to report to the German authorities who unjustly and hastily accused Spanish cucumbers, and specifically the cooperatives in Málaga and Almería, which were models of agricultural enterprises and will not recover from these accusations.
Thousands of unemployed families wonder why, why they need an answer, and above all, what their future will be, not only in Andalusia but throughout agricultural Europe.

Therefore, and trusting that experts will quickly find a solution, I demand that the Commission, when speaking with the Council today, request immediate and moral economic measures for all producers—from Andalusia and from throughout Europe—who are looking to us with indignation, yet also with hope.
Instead of promoting research and European collaboration, German authorities have slandered and harmed cucumber producers and others.

It is Germany that has a serious credibility problem.
It is unacceptable how Mrs. Merkel treats the countries of the south regarding agriculture and financial matters.
Someone said that Merkel was a disciple of Adenauer and Kohl.
None of that; it lacks credibility. Morality.
Mr. President, it is no coincidence that Spain, Italy, Greece, Ireland, Portugal, and some other countries share our hesitation to readily accept the so-called eurovignette.

The coincidence is that the burden stems from our condition as peripheral countries, and in the end we will be the ones who pay this unusual tax, which will be collected by the European Union states that hold geographic centrality.
The rationale underlying the eurovignette involves significant contradictions, and the supporting discourse grows increasingly fragile each day.

Although one may accept as a starting point the rationality of internalizing external costs, in the end it does not hold that, in order to alleviate congestion, only high-tonnage vehicles should be penalized while passenger cars are left unaffected, thereby applying the well-known principle that the polluter should pay in a clearly discriminatory manner.

On the other hand, ladies and gentlemen, the eurovignette, originally conceived as a source of funding for infrastructure, will ultimately allocate only 15 percent to the financing of trans-European networks, and that not obligatorily.

but as a mere recommendation, so that the incumbent Minister of Finance will allocate the collected funds to the purpose deemed most suitable for the country's interests within the broad scope of infrastructure.
The ultimate outcome will be nothing less than an additional obstacle to the transport of goods by road, which at present is the only means currently enabling the internal market.
This is the reality, ladies and gentlemen, a reality that is far harsher in the context of an economic crisis.

In this regard, the competitiveness of European products in global markets cannot afford experiments that might further deteriorate the already fragile state of the European economy through the imposition of additional costs.

The food crisis, which recklessly has been shifted onto Spanish products, has resulted in unjust penalties and massive losses for those it penalizes—freight transporters and farmers of premium crops—through the eurovignette.
Those are the reasons, Madam Presidents and Members, why the Spanish Delegation of the European People's Party will vote against this draft directive.

You, dear colleagues, will understand that, assuming the chair of this session, I express to you my sorrow and deep distress over the passing of Jorge Semprún, which occurred a few hours ago at the age of eighty-seven.

We also celebrate the outstanding committed writer, in particular a contributor to our program The European Book of the Year, and we also celebrate the convinced Europeanist politician.

I mourn his death also as his comrade and friend, and I will ask President Buzek to convey to his family and to the Government of Spain a message of heartfelt condolences and solidarity, which I am certain we all share here.

In defense of fundamental rights, I call on the responsible institutions to reconsider San Sebastián's designation as European Capital of Culture, as I believe it was selected on political grounds, thereby constituting discrimination against the other candidate cities.

The president of the Tribunal said she was chosen to support the peace process, and as far as I know, in Spain there exists a terrorist organization called ETA, which to this day has not abandoned arms, and which two months ago succeeded in enabling its political wing, Bildu, to govern, among other municipalities, the city of San Sebastián.

I believe in a culture of life, not in a culture of shooting children.

No and a thousand times no.
And when San Sebastián becomes a free city for all, let it reapply, and in the meantime, let them learn the culture of respect for those who think differently.
Because tomorrow they might deserve it, but today, with Batasuna and its heirs in office, they do not deserve it.

Madam President, from the European Parliament, I wish to pay tribute today to all women. Despite the significant progress we have made since that March of 1911, when women took to the streets demanding the right to vote and better working conditions, I believe true gender equality remains far from achieved.
Today women can work without having to ask permission from anyone, but in many cases they still earn less than men for similar work, which is unacceptable.
It is also necessary to remember the women in rural areas whose work goes unrecognized.
I believe another uncomfortable reality, yet one that must continue to be denounced, is violence against women.

This year alone, an excessive number of women have already been murdered, not counting the serious threats made by those who believe they have the right to inflict physical or psychological violence upon them.
It is time to say enough, and to say it loudly and clearly.
From this Parliament, I reaffirm my commitment to the European Protection Order for Victims and to the Victims' Directive presented by the Commission, which will be voted on, God willing, next June.
We must also say—and I would like to emphasize—that motherhood continues to be an obstacle to women's advancement, and I believe this trend should be changed.
I want to send a message of hope, and together with many women, with the growing support of men, we must advance along the path of equality of opportunities, rights, and also of responsibilities.
We have come a long way, but there is much still to be done.
I must acknowledge that I do not absolutely and deeply believe in quotas, but I must tell you that something needs to be done, and I agree with that.
many of my colleagues, and of course Mrs. Reding, in launching this debate and advancing along this path so that women can regain the place they deserve, and to that end you can count on my support.

Mr. President, at a time of economic crisis and budget cuts affecting public health—where, as in the community where I live, research centers such as the Prince Felipe Institute, specialized in diabetes, are left without funding—this resolution could not be more timely or appropriate.

We need a common European strategy against diabetes, and we need a recommendation from the Council stating that Member States must increase research, including research involving stem cells, must coordinate their findings to optimize results, and must establish programs promoting healthy eating and lifestyles.

In short, we must prioritize human health above all else, and in this case, investigating, coordinating, and implementing national programs coordinated by the Commission and demanding results on diabetes is to ensure the quality of life of Europeans and to safeguard their very lives.

If we allocate more funding to this, some people will receive payments slightly later, but certainly those currently facing difficulties will enjoy a better quality of life, and we will, as Member States, as the Council, and as the Commission, be fulfilling one of our obligations: safeguarding the health of our citizens.

The European social model, which we so proudly uphold, is not based solely on our pension, education, or healthcare systems, but also on a particular understanding of the balance between employer and employee within companies.

The labor reform approved by the Spanish Government without dialogue or consensus breaks with that social model and moves us away from the objectives of the 2020 Strategy, both in terms of job creation and in terms of job quality.
A labor reform alone does not create jobs; economic growth does.
What labor law does do is define the conditions under which workers enter companies, the conditions under which they remain in them, and the conditions under which they leave them.

And with this reform, where eighty percent of workers will be left unprotected because they will not be subject to collective agreements, where they could be dismissed due to a decline in company revenues.
that they may unilaterally modify their schedules, it is clear that workers will enter the labor market with greater precarity, remain in it with more fear, and exit it with reduced compensation.

That is why this reform, which undermines the European social model and seeks to enhance the competitiveness of the Spanish economy solely in relation to workers' conditions, deserves a general strike such as the one that must be called tomorrow in Spain in defense of this social model.

Mr. Commissioner, to govern is to set priorities, to govern is to set priorities, to take the initiative, and moreover, with the aim of achieving some goal, some objective—and moreover, the Commission is not doing so.

Governance is not merely carrying out a simple reallocation of funds, nor even doing so and then announcing it merely to cleanse one's image in the eyes of public opinion; rather, it means advancing an ambitious, enduring, and swift strategy to eradicate youth unemployment.

Governance does not mean resigning oneself to a youth unemployment rate above twenty percent and being content with a pilot project worth four million euros, which is absurd in light of the situation and the magnitude of the problem we face.

Governance does not mean giving in to the pressures from the College of Commissioners or from other Member States of the Council that strive to convince us that we will only emerge from this financial crisis, this new wave of recession, by surrendering wages, labor rights, or compensation, leaving us with no future but one of precariousness as a generation.

Because if this were true, Mr. Commissioner, that we only achieve competitiveness with precariousness, and that we can only become more competitive by being even more precarious, then Zimbabwe would be the most competitive country—and it is not.
Sweden is, Finland is, and Denmark is as well, precisely characterized by a model of harmonious labor relations and also by a model of guaranteed labor rights.
Mr. President, employment is much more than a civic right; it is a means of connection to the system, an instrument of social integration.

that silent and accepted crime endured by millions of women and girls around the world—seventy million, I repeat, seventy million

When people ask me in my home country what I do in Europe, I can proudly say that I am legislating in favor of victims.
And that work deeply honors me personally and gives me great strength to continue working.

I am confident that, with the support and commitment not only of those of us working here in Europe but also of our colleagues in our respective countries, we will, together—with the assistance of Your Lordships—successfully bring victims out of that unjust oblivion.

Mr. President, my colleague Turmes has done an admirable job, because to advance a regulation of this nature, it took the courage of this rapporteur, who has skillfully overcome the many obstacles that were carefully laid in the way.
It has been worthwhile because efficiency in energy use and its conservation are requirements of any energy policy worthy of the name.

We should commend significant measures such as the requirement for the renovation of public buildings, the imposition of an accumulated energy savings target on energy companies, as well as mandatory audits for large private enterprises.
Moreover, Turmes's and the other rapporteurs' efforts will be of no avail unless accompanied by decisive action from the Member States.
An action that, in turn, must be accompanied by a firm commitment to these objectives on the part of the regions, municipalities, and European citizens.
I am interested in highlighting the involvement of these latter individuals.
For now, what is evident is much frivolity among the authorities and among the citizens.

Mr. President, the trade agreement with Central America, Colombia, and Peru is an important agreement, and the safeguard clause aims, with the amendments we have proposed, to ensure it is simple, swift, and provides guarantees.

We have submitted amendments to protect European production in accordance with the Treaties, and we have established security mechanisms for both European production and production in the countries where the Agreement is implemented.
Protection of the outermost peripheral regions is necessary because they are not diversified in cultivation, due to economic factors, as they affect a large number of.
More than one hundred direct jobs and three hundred fifty indirect jobs in Spain, in a population heavily affected by unemployment and its specific circumstances.
For example, the Canary Islands banana has significant specificity in agriculture, culture, and gastronomy.

But it is also necessary there, in the regions where we will establish the Agreement—Central America, Colombia, and Peru—because trade is fundamental to development, and development entails labor rights, sustainability, and benefits for the producer.

Mr. President, the rapporteur's work is very good, but I would like to take this opportunity to say here, within this framework, that our prose is extremely unclear, as anyone who examines European legislation can readily observe.
We have forgotten what Pufendorf already taught, that general laws should be few in number and their meaning must be properly understood.
Therefore, those who promulgate it must use as much clarity as possible.
And Montesquieu always insisted that legal language must be precise and concise.
That is, we must avoid esoteric jargon, filled with neologisms and unjustified linguistic borrowings, because it creates a malignant web where traps thrive and arbitrariness takes root.
We deal with very complex issues in this Parliament, but it is our duty to convey them to citizens in a comprehensible and accessible manner.
It is common for legal professionals themselves—judges, lawyers—to become frustrated when confronted with our texts.
Good prose, dear colleagues, is the legislator's courtesy and a way of demonstrating respect for the citizen.
Honorable deputy, I have just spent the time—however little I had—insulting and discrediting you from the most complete ignorance.
ignorance. You are familiar with the Spanish Constitution.

Because what the Vice President, Mr. Vidal Cuadras, has stated is nothing more or less than applying what is stated in the Spanish Constitution. The rest are singular personal opinions that are entirely irrelevant, which I may respect but which I disdain.
Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, there exists an old principle that has moved from the legal to the political sphere, and it is pacta sunt servanda—agreements are made to be kept.
By this I mean the significant measures adopted by the European Council in June to address the crisis affecting us.

Since the adoption of these agreements, market pressures eased and the markets welcomed decisions that could break the vicious cycle between banks and sovereign debt, as well as the plans to advance toward a genuine economic and monetary union and the commitment to defend the euro in an irreversible manner.
The European Council held last week contains, ladies and gentlemen, undeniable positive aspects; it is satisfactory that the conclusions reflect progress toward banking union.

It is also positive that a consensus of twenty-seven has been reached regarding the establishment of the banking supervisor. Finally, it is appropriate that the implementation be entrusted to the Eurozone's finance ministers.
but they will allow him to conduct a critical examination of the biased interpretations that have been made of it in a unilateral manner.

Your Lordships, it is unacceptable to make particular interpretations that may distort the mandate granted to the Ministers of Economy, nor do we endorse recounting matters as agreements that were never even discussed.
It is urgent to move toward a single supervisory mechanism that must cover all financial institutions.
A common deposit guarantee scheme and a crisis resolution mechanism are also required.
The survival of the euro is linked to the urgent creation of an effective banking union.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, Commissioner, the advantages of the internal market are undeniable—increased competition, and a greater supply of products and services.
services, creation of new jobs, more affordable prices for consumers, and greater protection—still insufficient, and we cannot afford to become complacent.
However, cultural, linguistic, protectionist, and bureaucratic barriers prevent citizens and businesses from fully benefiting from the single market.
Half a year ago, we celebrated the launch of the twelve priorities aimed at stimulating growth and strengthening citizens' confidence in the internal market.
Unfortunately, an agreement has not yet been reached on most of the proposals, as the Commission itself acknowledged in last week's communication.

Madam President, Commissioner, the urgency of the crisis compels us to be ambitious, to go much further, and to achieve agreements as soon as possible in order to boost growth, employment, and the confidence of citizens and businesses in the single market.
Mr. President, I too would like to congratulate the rapporteur and the other colleagues who have worked on this reform very adequately.

The financial crisis has intensified due to the existence of complex and obscure tradable assets linked to derivative instruments and off-exchange transactions, all aimed at achieving high returns in a very short time.
It is necessary to bring financial operations into a transparent market to ensure accurate price formation and the competitiveness of tradable products.
But above all, it is essential that the information provided be understandable to investors, who must have full awareness in order to freely consent to the risks they undertake.
Transparency is of no use as a great flash of light if the obscurity surrounding the structuring of tradable products blinds citizens' understanding.
I like Mrs. Thyssen's report because I believe that anything which strengthens the role and authority of this Parliament in political debate is moving in the right direction.
We too often hear accusations against European institutions regarding their limited democratic quality.
It is a statement made casually, and this is not the occasion to enter into this debate.

Yes, to emphasize that this Parliament possesses the same legitimacy as any national parliament, and it is even likely that, with regard to some, it operates under more transparent rules and furthermore allows greater freedom to its members.
Therefore, all economic measures must pass through the filter represented by this Parliament before their final approval, and subsequently, when examining their implementation and outcomes.

Mr. President, fifty-three percent of the active population works in the automotive sector.
The figures speak for themselves.
The decline in sales in the European market, combined with corporate strategy, means that, in general terms, companies' externalization and restructuring
There is a crisis of overcapacity currently affecting ten assembly plants and potentially up to one hundred workers.

Unfortunately, there are few cases of restructuring with a long-term vision. Generally, a short-term perspective prevails, in which the main focus is gaining competitiveness through labor adjustments.

I must tell you that on the very same day that Ford communicated the suspension of the GEN plant, the Valencia Ford company was informed that its production would be moved to Germany, in the same corporate announcement.

Therefore, we believe this is a general issue for Europe, and we consider it essential to establish a legal framework at the European level that anticipates changes and underscores the importance of social dialogue.
Social dialogue is fundamental.
Mr. President, today we are debating four reports that represent a revitalization of our common policy on security and defense.
It is a clear indication of the conviction that our security and defense have a distinct European dimension, in contrast to the minority euro-skeptical factions that also prevail in this Chamber.
The report by Mr. Dan Jan, whom I would like to thank for his spirit of consensus-building throughout its preparation, contains significant contributions from the Socialist Group.
among others, references to effective multilateralism, the responsibility to protect principle, or the concept of human security.
And we would like these aspects to be included in the possible review of the European Defence Strategy.
We have also pointed out that a strong common security and defense policy is of interest to our transatlantic alliance as well.
I must highlight another challenge that is acquiring significant proportions for our global security.
It is that of cybersecurity and cyberdefense, a report which we also discussed today, and whose recommendations we hope will be included in the new European Cybersecurity Strategy.
In a context of severe economic and financial crisis, in which Member States are being forced to cut their defense budgets, it is precisely when European projects are most needed.
That is why it is more important than ever to strengthen the role of the European Defence Agency, which is currently facing significant criticism.
I conclude by stating that we can relaunch European security and defense, and do so with President Van Rompuy's project for the December 2013 Council.
Thank you very much to the four speakers.
Mr. President, Ladies and Gentlemen, once again, the defense of the general interests championed by the Commission and the Parliament confronts national selfishness that prevails in the Council.
Given the prior acceptance of the legitimacy of both positions, reaching a balance is inexcusable.
The relevance of the upcoming financial framework is unquestionable.
The Spanish delegation of the PPE and I, on its behalf, reiterate that, in our view, the Presidency's proposal is unacceptable.
A cut of seventy-five million euros from the already inevitably meager proposal by the European Commission proves sterile and useless.
And it is not only that the new cut directly to agricultural and cohesion policies goes beyond the red line that Spain has drawn since the beginning of the negotiations.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is no longer the time to debate a supposed dichotomy between austerity and growth.
Some of us are making proven efforts and sacrifices to correct the deficit.
But Spain, like many other countries, needs to grow, increase economic activity, and create jobs.
And with this budget, one achieves nothing. On the contrary, it serves as a missile to the line of flotation.
Mr. President, it is not possible to achieve more Europe with fewer resources, especially when those resources are distributed unjustly.

If that is the case, Spain will oppose a financial framework for the European Union that unequivocally eliminates prospects for growth and, above all, any trace of solidarity, and this is precisely the direction the European Council is taking.
Fortunately, this is not the path indicated by the Commission, to whom we must express our gratitude for its efforts in pointing out a different route.

And at this point, in the need for unity, in the need for shared efforts, I would like to thank Mr. Barroso for his firm statement, made the other day in Cádiz, interpreting the Treaties decisively in the case of a secessionist scenario within the Union.

He made it clear, unequivocally, as guardian of the Treaties, that if one part of a Member State separates as an independent state, it becomes regarded as a third country and, therefore, will not be subject to the provisions or effects of the Treaty itself.
I hope, Mr. Vice President, in the absence of the Chairman, that this may be taken up and that this safe and consistent doctrine of the Commission may continue to remain alive as assurance for all.
